chunk_summary: Weight faltering in a 7-month-old infant is linked to low energy density from thin gruels. Clinical management includes improving food texture, energy density, and diversity to meet complementary feeding needs.
learning_objectives: Understand the energy gap in infants aged 6–8 months and its implications for growth.
Identify and address inadequate energy density in complementary feeding practices.
Develop a dietary plan to improve weight velocity in infants with weight faltering.
Recognize the importance of texture, diversity, and nutrient density in complementary feeding.
key_facts: At 6–8 months, infants require ~200 kcal/day from complementary foods in addition to breast milk.
Thin gruels like rice water displace breastfeeds but fail to meet energy needs due to low energy density.
Weight faltering is defined as crossing down >1 centile space on growth charts.
Recommended dietary changes include thick porridge, added fats (1–2 tsp oil/ghee per bowl), and protein-rich foods like dal or egg.
Target feeding frequency: 2–3 meals/day plus 1 nutritious snack, alongside on-demand breastfeeding.
reference_quote: "At 6–8 months there is a predictable energy gap that must be filled by complementary foods (about 200 kcal/day in addition to breast milk)." — WHO Guiding Principles for Complementary Feeding
document_key_topics: Improving energy density and food diversity
